params.text:
Why safe moving and handling matters

Helping people to move is one of the most common things you will do in adult social care. It is also one of the most likely tasks to cause serious harm — both to you and to the person you support. Manual handling is behind a large share of workplace injuries in health and social care, and many of these injuries are avoidable.

  • Harm to staff: unsafe lifting and awkward postures cause back pain, strains and long-term musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) that can end careers and lead to time off work.
  • Harm to the person you care for: poor technique can bruise fragile skin, damage shoulders and joints, cause falls, and take away a person's dignity and confidence.

Good moving and handling protects everyone. It keeps you fit to work, keeps the people you support safe and comfortable, and helps them stay as independent as possible.

params.alt: TILE: assess task, individual, load and environment

params.text:
What this module is — and is not

This is an awareness module. It explains the law, the principles and the equipment so you understand why safe handling matters and what good practice looks like.

It is not a substitute for practical training. Safe technique — how to use a hoist, position a slide sheet or help someone stand — must be learned in person, under a qualified trainer who can watch you and correct you. Never attempt a moving-and-handling task you have not been trained and assessed as competent to do.

params.question: This online module is enough on its own to make me competent to operate a hoist.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Awareness is important, but you must complete practical, trainer-led training and be assessed as competent before using equipment like a hoist.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. This module builds awareness. Practical skills such as using a hoist must be taught and assessed by a qualified trainer before you carry them out.
